timeline.event: Born on October 27 in New York City, New York, USA, as the eldest son of Theodore Roosevelt Sr. and Martha Bulloch.
Graduated with honors from Harvard University and enrolled in Columbia Law School, but dropped out. Married Alice Hathaway Lee the same year.
Began his political career at age 24 as a member of the New York State Assembly.
His mother, Martha Bulloch, and his first wife, Alice Hathaway Lee, died.
Resumed political activities.
Appointed president of the board of New York City Police Commissioners, where he gained fame by reviving reform movements and exposing and punishing corrupt politicians.

When the Spanish-American War was declared, he resigned as Assistant Secretary of the Navy, formed the 'Rough Riders' regiment, and won a major victory at the Battle of Kettle Hill in Cuba. In the same year, he was commissioned as a colonel in the US Army, retired, and was elected Governor of New York.
Ran as the vice-presidential candidate on the ticket with President William McKinley and was elected.
Succeeded to the presidency upon McKinley's assassination, becoming the 26th President.

Intervened in the coal strike, setting a precedent for active government intervention, and passed legislation creating the federal Bureau of Corporations. Earned the nickname 'Trust Buster' by invoking the Sherman Antitrust Act to prosecute the Northern Securities Company.
Employed 'Big Stick' diplomacy when acquiring the Panama Canal Zone from Colombia.
Began drafting the 'Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine' with Elihu Root. Successfully re-elected in the presidential election.

As the Roosevelt Corollary began to take effect, he pressured the Dominican Republic government to appoint an American professional manager as a financial advisor. Mediated the Treaty of Portsmouth ending the Russo-Japanese War. Dispatched William Howard Taft to conclude the secret Taft-Katsura Agreement with Japan. Strengthened the authority of the Forest Service, canceling and revoking permits for dam construction projects.

Became the first sitting president to win the Nobel Peace Prize for his mediation in the Moroccan Crisis and his contribution to ending the Russo-Japanese War. Signed the 'Hepburn Act' granting the Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) the power to set maximum railroad rates. Signed the Pure Food and Drug Act. Persuaded Congress to grant the president the authority to convert remaining public lands into national forests.

Concluded the Gentlemen's Agreement with Japan to quell anti-Japanese protests in California. Signed the Root-Takahira Agreement, reaffirming America's rights in the Philippines in exchange for acknowledging Japan's dominance in Manchuria and Korea. Financial panic occurred.
Traveled through Africa and Europe for 10 months after leaving office in March.
Clashed with President Taft over Taft's authorization of an antitrust suit against U.S. Steel.
After failing to secure the Republican presidential nomination, he formed the Progressive Party and ran for president but was defeated. Survived being shot during a campaign speech.
Strongly opposed Wilson's League of Nations proposal. Attempted another presidential run, designating Warren Harding as his running mate. Hospitalized in October due to deteriorating health.
Died in his sleep from breathing difficulties at his home in Oyster Bay, New York, on January 6.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of America's First National Park
keyAchievements.description: Established the first national parks in the United States.

keyAchievements.title: Trust-Busting Using the Sherman Antitrust Act
keyAchievements.description: Prevented the harmful effects of monopolies by using the Sherman Antitrust Act to break up overly powerful trusts formed by large corporations.

keyAchievements.title: Awarded the Nobel Peace Prize
keyAchievements.description: Received the Nobel Peace Prize for his role in ending the Russo-Japanese War, becoming the first American and the first US President to receive the award.

keyAchievements.title: Support for the Establishment of the American Museum of Natural History
keyAchievements.description: Provided full administrative and financial support to ensure the establishment of the American Museum of Natural History in New York.

faq.question: Which president was Theodore Roosevelt?
faq.answer: He was the 26th President of the United States (1901–1909) and the 25th Vice President (1901).

faq.question: What was Theodore Roosevelt's nickname?
faq.answer: His nickname was Teddy, and he was also referred to as Theodore or Theodore Roosevelt.

faq.question: Did Theodore Roosevelt win the Nobel Peace Prize?
faq.answer: Yes, in 1906, he became the first sitting president to win the Nobel Peace Prize, awarded for his mediation in the Moroccan Crisis and his efforts to end the Russo-Japanese War.

faq.question: Is the teddy bear related to Theodore Roosevelt?
faq.answer: Yes. In 1902, after a news story reported that President Roosevelt had refused to shoot a bear cub during a hunting trip, a New York toy store owner named a stuffed bear 'Teddy' in his honor, originating the 'teddy bear'.

timeline.event: Born Frederick Augustus Washington Bailey in Talbot County, Maryland.
His mother, Harriet Bailey, dies (Frederick is 9).
Sophia Auld, the wife of Hugh Auld, teaches him to read (Frederick is 10).
Returns to a plantation in Talbot County, is rented out to 'slave breaker' Edward Covey, and fights back against a beating.
Returns to Fells Point, joins the East Baltimore Mental Improvement Society, and meets Anna Murray.
Escapes from slavery to freedom on September 3; marries Anna Murray on September 15; moves to New Bedford, Massachusetts, and changes his name to Frederick Douglass.
Is inspired by hearing William Lloyd Garrison speak at a meeting of the Bristol Anti-Slavery Society; gives his first speech at a Massachusetts Anti-Slavery Society convention in Nantucket.
Participates in the American Anti-Slavery Society's Hundred Conventions project, touring the eastern and midwestern United States.
Is beaten by a mob in Pendleton, Indiana, breaking his hand.
Publishes his first book, 'Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave', and leaves for a tour of Ireland.
Returns to the United States, settles in Rochester, New York, and founds the newspaper 'The North Star'.
Attends the Seneca Falls Convention and signs the Declaration of Sentiments.
Merges 'The North Star' with Gerrit Smith's 'Liberty Party Paper' to form 'Frederick Douglass' Paper'.
Publishes 'My Bondage and My Freedom'.
Returns from England following the death of his youngest daughter, Annie, at age 11 in Rochester.
Meets with President Lincoln regarding the treatment of Black soldiers.
Supports Ulysses S. Grant's presidential campaign.
Moves to Washington, D.C., after his home in Rochester burns down.
Purchases his final home, 'Cedar Hill', in Washington, D.C.
Appointed Recorder of Deeds for the District of Columbia.
His wife, Anna, passes away.
Marries Helen Pitts, a white suffragist.
Travels to England, France, Italy, Egypt, and Greece with his new wife, Helen Pitts.
Publishes 'Life and Times of Frederick Douglass'; appointed Haitian commissioner to the World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago.
Dies in Washington, D.C., on February 20.

keyAchievements.title: Leading Figure in the Abolitionist Movement
keyAchievements.description: Was a central figure in the abolition of slavery in the United States, advocating based on his experiences as a slave and his biblical convictions.

keyAchievements.title: Influential Orator and Author
keyAchievements.description: Known as the 'Sage of Anacostia', he is considered one of the most influential orators and authors in American history.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of His Autobiography
keyAchievements.description: Published 'Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave' in 1845, which became an immediate bestseller and helped expose the realities of slavery.

keyAchievements.title: Newspaper Publisher
keyAchievements.description: Published several newspapers, including 'The North Star', founded in 1847, to advocate for abolition and civil rights.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Women's Suffrage Movement
keyAchievements.description: Supported women's equality by attending the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848 and signing the Declaration of Sentiments.

keyAchievements.title: Meetings with Presidents
keyAchievements.description: Was the first Black man to meet with President Abraham Lincoln (to discuss the treatment of Black soldiers) and President Andrew Johnson (to discuss Black suffrage).

keyAchievements.title: Holding Political Office
keyAchievements.description: Held several significant political positions after the Civil War, including President of the Freedman's Savings Bank, Marshal of the District of Columbia, and Minister to Haiti.

faq.question: What was Frederick Douglass's original name?
faq.answer: His original name was Frederick Augustus Washington Bailey.

faq.question: Why did he change his name?
faq.answer: After escaping slavery, he changed his name to Frederick Douglass, taking the surname from a heroic character in Sir Walter Scott's poem 'The Lady of the Lake'.

faq.question: What is his most famous book?
faq.answer: His 1845 autobiography, 'Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave'.

faq.question: What was his view on the U.S. Constitution?
faq.answer: He initially believed the Constitution was pro-slavery, but later changed his view, concluding that it was an anti-slavery document that could be used as a weapon in the fight against slavery.

faq.question: What was his stance on racial equality?
faq.answer: He firmly believed in the equality of all people—Black, female, immigrant, etc.—and spent his life advocating for the brotherhood of all mankind.

faq.question: Who was his second wife?
faq.answer: His second wife was Helen Pitts, a white suffragist and the daughter of his fellow abolitionist Gideon Pitts Jr.

timeline.event: Marriage of her parents, Harriet 'Rit' Green and Ben Ross
Born Araminta 'Minty' Ross (estimated birth year)
Her father, Ben Ross, is freed from slavery according to a former owner's will
Marries John Tubman
Escapes to Philadelphia following deteriorating health
Escapes with her brothers Ben and Henry, but the brothers turn back
Rescues her niece Kessiah and Kessiah's two children
Returns to Maryland and rescues her brother Moses and two other enslaved people
Returns to Dorchester County to find her husband John, but he has remarried and refuses to leave with her
Leads a group of 11 escaped slaves to Canada
Her father Ben and his brother buy her mother Rit from Eliza Brodess for $20
Rescues her elderly parents and brings them to St. Catharines, Ontario, Canada
Is introduced to the abolitionist John Brown
John Brown reveals his plan to raid Harpers Ferry, Virginia, in Chatham-Kent, Ontario
Buys a small piece of land on the outskirts of Auburn, New York, from Senator William H. Seward for $1,200
Undertakes her final rescue mission, bringing the Ennals family and another group to freedom
Arrives with the Ennals family and others at the home of David and Martha Coffin Wright in Auburn
Heads to the Hilton Head area of South Carolina after the outbreak of the Civil War
President Lincoln issues the Emancipation Proclamation
Executes the Combahee River Raid, rescuing over 700 enslaved people
Stays at a military base for several months after the Confederate surrender before returning home
Marries Nelson Davis
Sarah Bradford publishes the biography 'Scenes in the Life of Harriet Tubman'
Gets caught up in a gold-smuggling scam
Adopts a baby girl named Gertie
Sarah Bradford publishes 'Harriet, the Moses of Her People'
Undergoes brain surgery at Massachusetts General Hospital in Boston
Participates as a keynote speaker at the first meeting of the National Federation of Afro-American Women
Begins receiving a pension for her military service during the Civil War
Donates a parcel of her land to the African Methodist Episcopal Zion Church
Attends the opening of the Harriet Tubman Home for the Aged as an honorary guest
Enters the nursing home named after her
Dies of pneumonia
Earl Conrad publishes 'Harriet Tubman: Negro Soldier and Abolitionist'
The United States Maritime Commission launches the SS Harriet Tubman, its first Liberty ship named after a Black woman
The US Postal Service issues the Tubman commemorative stamp, the first to honor an African American
Molefi Kete Asante includes Harriet Tubman on his list of '100 Greatest African Americans'
Jean Humez publishes a book about Tubman's life
Kate Larson and Catherine Clinton publish biographies of Tubman
Towson University names a new campus residence hall 'Tubman House'
US Treasury Secretary Jack Lew announces that Tubman's portrait will replace Andrew Jackson's on the $20 bill
Posthumously commissioned as a brigadier general in the Maryland National Guard on Veterans Day

keyAchievements.title: Activism in the Abolitionist Movement
keyAchievements.description: Through the 'Underground Railroad', a network of anti-slavery activists, she established 13 safe houses and rescued around 70 enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as an 'Underground Railroad' Conductor
keyAchievements.description: As a 'conductor' on the Underground Railroad from 1850 to 1860, she ventured into the South and led over 300 Black people to freedom in the North.

keyAchievements.title: Rescuing Family
keyAchievements.description: After escaping to Philadelphia, she returned to Maryland to lead her relatives out of the state, eventually bringing freedom to dozens of enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in the Civil War
keyAchievements.description: Served the Union Army as a cook, nurse, armed scout, and spy.

keyAchievements.title: Commanding the Combahee River Raid
keyAchievements.description: Became the first woman to lead an armed assault during the Civil War, commanding the Combahee River Raid and freeing over 700 enslaved people.

keyAchievements.title: Women's Suffrage Movement
keyAchievements.description: Fought for women's suffrage after the war, attending related conferences and speaking out in cities like New York, Boston, and Washington D.C.

keyAchievements.title: Contribution to the Establishment of a Home for the Aged
keyAchievements.description: Spent her final years in a home for elderly African Americans that she helped establish years earlier. In 1903, she donated part of her land to the church, requesting it be used for a home for 'aged and indigent colored people'.

faq.question: What was Harriet Tubman's original name?
faq.answer: Harriet Tubman's birth name was Araminta Ross.

faq.question: How many enslaved people did Harriet Tubman rescue?
faq.answer: She directly rescued around 70 people in 13 trips via the Underground Railroad, led over 300 to freedom in the North as a 'conductor' between 1850 and 1860, and freed more than 700 through the Combahee River Raid.

faq.question: Was there a bounty on Harriet Tubman?
faq.answer: There is a rumor of a $40,000 bounty on her head, but this is untrue. Even claims of a $12,000 bounty lack supporting evidence.

faq.question: What role did Harriet Tubman play in the Civil War?
faq.answer: She served the Union Army as a cook, nurse, armed scout, and spy. She was the first woman to lead an armed assault, freeing over 700 enslaved people in the Combahee River Raid.

faq.question: Why was Harriet Tubman nicknamed 'Moses'?
faq.answer: As she guided more and more people to freedom from slavery, she became famously known as 'Moses', drawing a parallel to the biblical figure in Exodus who liberated his oppressed people.

faq.question: Will Harriet Tubman appear on the $20 bill?
faq.answer: On April 20, 2016, US Treasury Secretary Jack Lew announced that Tubman's portrait would replace former President Andrew Jackson's on the front of the $20 bill.

timeline.event: Born on June 14 in Litchfield, Connecticut, USA.
Lived in Cincinnati, Ohio.
Wrote and published a children's geography book under her sister Catharine's name.
Married Calvin Stowe, a seminary professor.
Published the anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'.
Published the novel 'Dred'.
Published the novel 'The Minister's Wooing'.
Published the novel 'The Pearl of Orr's Island'.
Published 'Lady Byron Vindicated'.
Published 'Sam Lawson's Oldtown Fireside Stories'.
Published the novel 'Oldtown Folks'.
Died on July 1 at the age of 85 in Hartford.

keyAchievements.title: Writing 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'
keyAchievements.description: Published the anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin', which intensified the conflict of opinion between the North and South, ultimately leading to the Civil War.

keyAchievements.title: Writing a Children's Geography Book
keyAchievements.description: Wrote and published her first book, a children's geography book, in 1833 under her sister Catharine's name.

faq.question: Who was Harriet Beecher Stowe?
faq.answer: She was an American abolitionist and realist author, best known for her anti-slavery novel 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'.

faq.question: What was the impact of 'Uncle Tom's Cabin'?
faq.answer: The novel profoundly influenced the debate on slavery at the time and significantly widened the divide between the North and South, paving the way for the Civil War.

faq.question: Why did Harriet Beecher Stowe become interested in the abolitionist movement?
faq.answer: She witnessed the miserable living conditions of enslaved Black people during a trip to Kentucky, which fueled her passion for the abolitionist movement. Furthermore, the loss of her mother at a young age is said to have evoked a deep sense of empathy within her for enslaved boys and girls separated from their families.

timeline.event: Born as the only son of Kim Sun-yeong and Gwak Nak-won in Haeju, Hwanghae Province (birth name Kim Chang-am).
Took the Gwageo (state examination) at age 17 but failed.
Joined the Donghak movement and changed his name from Chang-am to Chang-su.
Participated in the Donghak Peasant Revolution, leading the Donghak army in Palbong, Haeju.
Sought refuge with An Tae-hun following the failure of the Donghak Peasant Revolution.
Murdered the Japanese national Tsuchida Josuke in Chihapo, Hwanghae Province, and was arrested.
While in prison, read books like the 'Great Learning' and 'World History' and taught fellow inmates how to read and write.
Succeeded in breaking out of prison with fellow inmates, became a Buddhist monk at Magoksa Temple in Gongju, and received the Buddhist name Wonjong.
Left Magoksa Temple and wandered before returning to secular life at Yeongcheonam Hermitage in Pyongyang and heading back to his hometown.
Worked as a village school teacher (hunjang) on Ganghwa Island, established a school in his hometown, and converted to Methodism after the mourning period for his father ended.
Established Bongyang School in Jangyeon, Hwanghae Province, and worked as a public school teacher and an agricultural committee member.
Engaged in movements to restore national sovereignty, including protests against the Eulsa Treaty.
Became a teacher at Seomyeonguisuk in Munhwa County, Hwanghae Province, and married Choi Jun-rye.
Joined the Sinminhoe (New People's Association) and served as the chief of its Hwanghae Province branch.
At age 36, changed his name from Gu (龜) to Gu (九).
Changed his name to Gu to remove himself from the Japanese family register, and adopted the pen name Baekbeom.
Implicated in the 105-Man Incident, sentenced to 15 years in prison, and incarcerated in Incheon Prison and Seodaemun Prison.
Granted special parole in August.

Went into exile in Shanghai, China, shortly after the March 1st Movement; participated in the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea, serving as a member of the Provisional Assembly and the Director of the Police Bureau.
Re-elected to the Provisional Assembly, appointed Minister of Home Affairs in the Provisional Government, organized the Korean Veterans Association, and became its first chairman.
Reappointed as Minister of Home Affairs, became acting Premier, and issued an order to dissolve the National Representative Conference.

His wife, Choi Jun-rye, passed away in Shanghai; directed the assassination of pro-Japanese collaborators and fundraising for military activities through figures like Park Hui-gwang of the Korean Independence Army (Daehan Tonguibu).
Elected President (Gungmuryeong) by the Provisional Assembly in December.
Resigned en masse from the presidency, was elected the first Chairman of the State Council, and appointed Minister of Home Affairs.
Organized the Korean Independence Party, was elected to its board of directors, and was reappointed as President.
Formed the Korean Patriotic Organization to assassinate Japanese officials and key figures.
Directed the Sakuradamon Incident by Lee Bong-chang and the Hongkou Park Incident by Yun Bong-gil.
Published a statement in Shanghai newspapers claiming responsibility for the bombings, escaped from Shanghai, and began life in hiding.
Supported the assassination of Ok Gwan-bin, met with Chiang Kai-shek, and agreed to establish a Korean training class at the Luoyang Military Academy.
Organized the Korean Special Forces Independence Army in Nanjing.
Opposed calls to dissolve the Provisional Government, founded the Korean National Party, and was elected its chairman.
Shot and injured by Lee Wun-han at Nanmu Hall in Changsha, Hunan Province.
His mother, Gwak Nak-won, passed away.
Elected Chairman (Juseok) of the Provisional Government in March, organized the Korean Liberation Army in September, and held a founding ceremony.
Sent a letter to US President Roosevelt requesting recognition of the Provisional Government, promulgated the Founding Principles of the Republic of Korea, and declared war on Japan.

Re-elected as Chairman of the Provisional Government, regained the right of supreme command over the Korean Liberation Army from the Republic of China, and inaugurated as Chairman of its Supreme Command.
After liberation, promoted movements to recognize the legitimacy of the Provisional Government, opposed trusteeship, and protested against the US-Soviet Joint Commission.
Visited Pyongyang to participate in North-South negotiations for establishing a unified government; however, after separate elections in the South, he devoted himself to educating the youth.
Assassinated by Ahn Doo-hee at his residence, Gyeonggyojang, on June 26.
Posthumously awarded the 'Order of Merit for National Foundation, Republic of Korea Medal'.

keyAchievements.title: Participation and Leadership in the Donghak Peasant Revolution
keyAchievements.description: Participated in the Donghak Peasant Revolution in 1894 and led the Donghak army in Palbong, Haeju.

keyAchievements.title: Chihapo Incident
keyAchievements.description: Murdered the Japanese national Tsuchida Josuke in Chihapo, Hwanghae Province, in 1896 to avenge the assassination of Empress Myeongseong.

keyAchievements.title: Patriotic Enlightenment Movement and Sinminhoe Activities
keyAchievements.description: Devoted himself to educational activities, such as establishing schools in the early 1900s, and served as the head of the Hwanghae Province branch of the Sinminhoe (New People's Association), a secret organization for restoring national sovereignty, in 1907.

keyAchievements.title: Holding Key Positions in the Provisional Government
keyAchievements.description: Served in major roles within the Provisional Government, including Assembly Member, Police Bureau Director, Home Affairs Minister, President, and Chairman, defending its legitimacy starting from 1919.

keyAchievements.title: Organizing the Korean Patriotic Organization and Directing Anti-Japanese Actions
keyAchievements.description: Formed the Korean Patriotic Organization in 1931 and directed the assassination attempts by Lee Bong-chang in Tokyo and Yun Bong-gil in Hongkou Park.

keyAchievements.title: Creation and Command of the Korean Liberation Army
keyAchievements.description: Organized the Korean Liberation Army, the first official military force of the Provisional Government, in 1940, and led it after becoming Chairman of its Supreme Command in 1944.

keyAchievements.title: Enactment of the Founding Principles and Declaration of War on Japan
keyAchievements.description: Promulgated the Founding Principles of the Republic of Korea and declared war on Japan in 1941, proclaiming the nation's resolve for independence.

keyAchievements.title: Participation in North-South Negotiations and Dedication to Youth Education
keyAchievements.description: Visited Pyongyang in 1948 to participate in North-South negotiations for a unified government, and subsequently devoted himself to youth education.

faq.question: What were Kim Gu's original name and pen names?
faq.answer: His original name was Chang-am. He changed it to Chang-su at age 19, and then from Gu (龜) to Gu (九) at age 36 (in 1912). His pen names were Baekbeom and Yeonsang.

faq.question: How did he come up with the pen name 'Baekbeom'?
faq.answer: The pen name 'Baekbeom' was created by taking 'Baek' (白) from 'baekjeong' (butcher), symbolizing the lowest class of people, and 'beom' (凡) from 'beombu' (commoner), symbolizing ordinary people.

faq.question: Who did Kim Gu kill in the Chihapo Incident, and why?
faq.answer: In 1896, Kim Gu murdered a Japanese man named Tsuchida Josuke in Chihapo, Hwanghae Province. He stated the reason was to avenge the death of Empress Myeongseong, the mother of the nation.

faq.question: What were Kim Gu's major roles in the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea?
faq.answer: Kim Gu served in various capacities, including member of the Provisional Assembly, Director of the Police Bureau, Minister of Home Affairs, acting Premier, President (Gungmuryeong), State Councilor, and Chairman of the State Council (Juseok) from 1940 to 1947.

faq.question: What is the most famous independence organization founded by Kim Gu?
faq.answer: In 1931, Kim Gu organized the Korean Patriotic Organization (Hanin Aegukdan) and directed the patriotic deeds of Lee Bong-chang and Yun Bong-gil.

faq.question: When and where did Kim Gu pass away?
faq.answer: Kim Gu was assassinated by Ahn Doo-hee at his residence, Gyeonggyojang, on June 26, 1949.
